title: Eating Disorders
oneLiner: Severe psychiatric disorders (anorexia nervosa, bulimia nervosa) causing medical instability via starvation, purging, and electrolyte disturbances — high mortality if missed.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Refeeding syndrome:** The single finding is a **rapid drop in serum phosphate (<0.5 mmol/L)** within 12–48 hours of starting feeding. This causes arrhythmia, respiratory failure, and seizures. Must suspect before starting any nutrition.
- **Cardiac arrhythmia (torsades de pointes):** QTc >500 ms on ECG is the threshold. Patients with anorexia often have prolonged QTc from electrolyte depletion; even mild hypokalaemia or hypomagnesaemia can precipitate fatal VT.
- **Severe hypoglycaemia:** Blood glucose <2.2 mmol/L without clear cause. Can present with reduced consciousness or seizures. Often occurs in advanced starvation due to depleted glycogen stores and low gluconeogenesis.
- **Suicidal attempt / overdose:** Eating disorders have high suicide risk; any collapse or unexplained sedation may be overdose (e.g., purgatives, insulin, antidepressants).
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Low body weight (BMI <18.5, often <15 in severe anorexia)
  - Preoccupation with weight/food, fear of weight gain
  - Purging behaviours (vomiting, laxatives, diuretics)
  - Amenorrhoea (in female adolescents), loss of libido
  - Exercise compulsion, restriction of food groups
  - Bradycardia, hypotension, hypothermia
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Lanugo (fine downy hair on face, trunk)
  - Salivary gland hypertrophy (bulimia – parotid enlargement)
  - Russell’s sign (knuckle calluses from inducing vomiting)
  - Cold peripheries, acrocyanosis
  - Muscle wasting, peripheral oedema (from hypoalbuminaemia or refeeding)
  - Dental erosion (posterior teeth, from gastric acid)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely:** Anorexia nervosa (restricting type) – low BMI, fear of weight gain.
- **Also likely:** Bulimia nervosa – normal or slightly overweight, recurrent binges + purges.
- **Medical causes of weight loss:**
  - Malignancy (constitutional symptoms, mass, bleeding)
  - Hyperthyroidism (tachycardia, tremor, goitre)
  - Inflammatory bowel disease (abdominal pain, diarrhoea, raised CRP)
  - Addison’s disease (hyperpigmentation, postural hypotension, hyponatraemia)
  - Diabetes mellitus (polyuria, polydipsia, ketones)
- **Psychiatric differential:** Depression (weight loss without body image distortion); ARFID (avoidant/restrictive food intake disorder – no fear of weight gain).
- **Splitter:** **Bradycardia + hypotension + lanugo** strongly point to anorexia nervosa rather than organic cachexia.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - ECG: calculate QTc (normal <450 ms, high risk if >500 ms)
  - Lying/standing BP (postural drop >20 systolic suggests volume depletion)
  - Temperature (hypothermia is a severity marker)
  - Weigh patient in light clothing – do not allow rituals (e.g., empty pockets)
  - Urinalysis: dip for ketones (starvation), pH (vomiting → alkaluria)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (anaemia, leukopenia)
  - U&E (hypokalaemia, hyponatraemia, metabolic alkalosis from vomiting)
  - Bone profile (hypocalcaemia, raised ALP from osteoporosis)
  - Magnesium, phosphate (low pre-referral or falling)
  - Glucose (check for hypoglycaemia)
  - LFTs (mildly raised transaminases – steatosis)
  - Thyroid function (euthyroid sick syndrome – low T3)
  - Cortisol (random >500 nmol/L suggests stress; avoid suppressing with dexamethasone unless Addison’s suspected)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Chest X-ray if suspicion of aspiration or if severely underweight (rib fracture, infection). DEXA scan only if chronic illness (>1 year).
  - **Second-line:** Bone age (if young and growth retardation). Brain MRI only if atypical features (e.g., neurological signs).
- **Special tests:** If purging suspected: stool laxative screen, serum carotene (raised in anorexia from excessive vegetable intake).
- **Diagnostic criteria (DSM-5 summary):**
  - Restriction of energy intake leading to significantly low body weight
  - Intense fear of gaining weight / persistent behaviour to prevent weight gain
  - Disturbance in self-perception of weight/shape; lack of recognition of seriousness of low weight
  - For bulimia: recurrent binge episodes + purging, at least once weekly for 3 months
- **Never-miss test:** **ECG** – every patient with anorexia or bulimia must have QTc measurement before any feeding or electrolyte replacement.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Assess ABC: airway usually fine, but check for aspiration if vomiting/unconscious
  - Secure IV access if unstable (HR <40, BP <70/40, GCS <15, hypoglycaemia)
  - Treat hypoglycaemia: give 50 mL 50% dextrose IV (check K+ first if possible) or 200 mL 10% dextrose if peripheral line only
  - Correct severe electrolyte disturbances:
    - Hypokalaemia (K+ <3.0): IV potassium with cardiac monitoring; max rate 10 mmol/h (central line if >10 mmol/h)
    - Hypomagnesaemia (Mg <0.5): IV magnesium 2–4 mmol/h (e.g., magnesium sulphate 50% 10 mL in 100 mL saline over 1 hour)
    - Hypophosphataemia (PO4 <0.5): IV phosphate 10–20 mmol over 6–12 hours (e.g., sodium phosphate 20 mmol in 500 mL 0.45% saline)
  - Admit to medical ward if:
    - BMI <13
    - Heart rate <40 bpm
    - QTc >450 ms ( >500 ms if symptomatic)
    - Rapid weight loss (>1 kg/week)
    - Electrolyte disturbance requiring monitoring
    - Severe hypotension or hypothermia
    - Suicidal ideation or intention
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - **Refeeding protocol** – start cautiously:
    - Begin at 5–10 kcal/kg/day (max 1000 kcal/d initially); increase by 200–300 kcal every 2–3 days
    - Monitor phosphate, Mg, K+ every 6 hours for first 48 hours, then daily
    - Prophylactic phosphate supplementation (unless level >1.2 mmol/L): e.g., Sandoz Phosphate 500 mg PO/NG QDS; adjust based on levels
  - Daily weight (same time, same clothes, empty bladder)
  - Strict fluid balance – avoid fluid overload (risk of peripheral oedema, heart failure)
  - ECG daily until QTc stable <450 ms
  - Involve liaison psychiatry / eating disorders team within 24 hours
  - Consider NG feeding if patient refuses oral intake and BMI <13 or rapid decline – but only with consent (or MHA if capacity lost)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Specialist multidisciplinary care (dietitian, psychologist, physician)
  - Cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT-E) for bulimia; family-based treatment for adolescents with anorexia
  - Monitoring of bone density if amenorrhoea >6 months; consider calcium + vitamin D (1000–1200 mg/day Ca, 800 IU vitamin D)
  - Gradual supervised refeeding until healthy weight achieved
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** If clinically dehydrated (postural BP drop, dry mucous membranes) – oral fluids only unless unable to swallow; avoid IV fluids if possible due to risk of overload. If IV required: 0.9% saline at 50–100 mL/h (max 1.5 L/d) with strict monitoring
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Assess risk – low BMI is NOT protective; many guidelines recommend prophylactic LMWH (e.g., enoxaparin 20 mg SC daily) if immobile or previous VTE, but weigh bleeding risk (coagulopathy from liver dysfunction). Follow local policy.
  - **Diet / NBM status:** Start oral intake as soon as safe (low-calorie, frequent small meals). NBM only if severe gastrointestinal symptoms (gastric dilatation, pancreatitis).
  - **Observation frequency:** 1–2 hourly observations for first 12 hours (HR, BP, temp, glucose), then 4 hourly once stable.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line for bulimia (if indicated after medical stabilisation):** [Prescribe only under specialist guidance]
  - **Fluoxetine** 20–60 mg PO once daily (NICE recommends 60 mg daily for bulimia; start at 20 mg and increase weekly). Check ECG before starting (QTc risk if severe electrolyte disturbance).
- **Common alternatives:**
  - **Olanzapine** (2.5–10 mg PO nocte) – may be used off-licence for weight gain in anorexia; monitor glucose and lipids.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Avoid SSRIs if QTc >500 ms or untreated electrolyte abnormality
  - Olanzapine: avoid in patients with baseline dysrhythmia or prolonged QTc
  - MAOIs: absolutely contraindicated in patients with impulsivity (risk of overdose)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Fluoxetine: reduced clearance in hepatic impairment – start 10 mg daily
  - Olanzapine: dose adjustment not usually needed but avoid in severe liver disease
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Starting a high dose of IV potassium without checking Mg first** – hypomagnesaemia prevents K+ stabilisation and risks arrhythmia.
  - **Starting refeeding without prophylactic phosphate** – precipitates refeeding syndrome.
  - **Giving a benzodiazepine for agitation to a severely underweight patient** – risk of respiratory depression.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Refeeding syndrome (life-threatening)
  - Cardiac arrhythmias (VT, torsades, bradycardia)
  - Hypokalaemia / hypomagnesaemia / hypophosphataemia
  - Gastric dilatation / rupture (especially after binge-purge)
  - Pancreatitis, aspiration pneumonia
  - Seizures (hypoglycaemia, electrolyte imbalance)
- **Chronic:**
  - Osteoporosis / osteopenia (especially in anorexia with amenorrhoea)
  - Infertility, growth retardation (if adolescent)
  - Dental erosion, loss of teeth (bulimia)
  - Chronic kidney disease (from hypokalaemia, hypovolaemia)
  - Oesophageal rupture (Mallory-Weiss tear) from repeated vomiting
sections.Prognosis:
- Anorexia nervosa: mortality 5–10% over 10 years (one of the highest psychiatric mortality rates); about 50% recover fully, 20–30% remain partial, 10–20% chronic severe.
- Bulimia: lower mortality (~1% per year), better recovery rates with early treatment (70% remission over 5 years).
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Start the refeeding protocol before correcting all electrolytes** – do not delay feeding while correcting hypophosphataemia; use parallel supplementation.
- **Always order an ECG and measure QTc before starting any medication** (including SSRIs, antiemetics – avoid ondansetron as it prolongs QTc).
- **Check orthostatic BP in every patient** – a drop >20 mmHg is a marker of volume depletion and need for careful fluid management.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Never start high-calorie refeeding (e.g., full meal plan) in severe anorexia** – that precipitates refeeding syndrome; always start low (5–10 kcal/kg).
- **Do not ignore bradycardia (HR 40–50) as “normal” in anorexia** – it is a sign of relative adrenal insufficiency and can decompensate with stress.
- **Do not discharge a patient with a QTc >500 ms** – they need continuous ECG monitoring and senior review until resolved.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Palpitations, syncope, or near-syncope (risk of arrhythmia)
  - Inability to keep down any food/bland fluids for >6 hours
  - Severe abdominal pain or vomiting blood (Mallory-Weiss or rupture)
  - Worsening suicidal thoughts or self-harm
- **Follow-up:**
  - Liaison with local eating disorders team within 1–2 weeks
  - GP review of weight, mood, and ECG (if electrolyte abnormalities were present)
  - Dietetic appointment for structured meal plan
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call crash team if:** NEWS2 ≥7, GCS <9, HR <35, BP <60 systolic, new onset arrhythmia (e.g., torsades)
- **Call senior (Reg/Consultant) immediately if:**
  - QTc >500 ms (or any QTc prolongation >460 ms with symptoms)
  - Phosphate <0.5 mmol/L or rapidly declining despite supplementation
  - Seizure, loss of consciousness, or suspected refeeding syndrome
  - Suspected gastric rupture (surgical emergency)
  - Suicidal intent or attempt (liaise psychiatry)
- **General thresholds:**
  - NEWS2 >5 or lactate >2 mmol/L with no clear cause
  - GCS drop by ≥2 points
  - Heart rate <40 or >130 (in the context of starvation)

rawMarkdown:
## Eating Disorders

**One-liner:** Severe psychiatric disorders (anorexia nervosa, bulimia nervosa) causing medical instability via starvation, purging, and electrolyte disturbances — high mortality if missed.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Refeeding syndrome:** The single finding is a **rapid drop in serum phosphate (<0.5 mmol/L)** within 12–48 hours of starting feeding. This causes arrhythmia, respiratory failure, and seizures. Must suspect before starting any nutrition.
- **Cardiac arrhythmia (torsades de pointes):** QTc >500 ms on ECG is the threshold. Patients with anorexia often have prolonged QTc from electrolyte depletion; even mild hypokalaemia or hypomagnesaemia can precipitate fatal VT.
- **Severe hypoglycaemia:** Blood glucose <2.2 mmol/L without clear cause. Can present with reduced consciousness or seizures. Often occurs in advanced starvation due to depleted glycogen stores and low gluconeogenesis.
- **Suicidal attempt / overdose:** Eating disorders have high suicide risk; any collapse or unexplained sedation may be overdose (e.g., purgatives, insulin, antidepressants).

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Low body weight (BMI <18.5, often <15 in severe anorexia)
  - Preoccupation with weight/food, fear of weight gain
  - Purging behaviours (vomiting, laxatives, diuretics)
  - Amenorrhoea (in female adolescents), loss of libido
  - Exercise compulsion, restriction of food groups
  - Bradycardia, hypotension, hypothermia
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Lanugo (fine downy hair on face, trunk)
  - Salivary gland hypertrophy (bulimia – parotid enlargement)
  - Russell’s sign (knuckle calluses from inducing vomiting)
  - Cold peripheries, acrocyanosis
  - Muscle wasting, peripheral oedema (from hypoalbuminaemia or refeeding)
  - Dental erosion (posterior teeth, from gastric acid)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely:** Anorexia nervosa (restricting type) – low BMI, fear of weight gain.
- **Also likely:** Bulimia nervosa – normal or slightly overweight, recurrent binges + purges.
- **Medical causes of weight loss:**
  - Malignancy (constitutional symptoms, mass, bleeding)
  - Hyperthyroidism (tachycardia, tremor, goitre)
  - Inflammatory bowel disease (abdominal pain, diarrhoea, raised CRP)
  - Addison’s disease (hyperpigmentation, postural hypotension, hyponatraemia)
  - Diabetes mellitus (polyuria, polydipsia, ketones)
- **Psychiatric differential:** Depression (weight loss without body image distortion); ARFID (avoidant/restrictive food intake disorder – no fear of weight gain).
- **Splitter:** **Bradycardia + hypotension + lanugo** strongly point to anorexia nervosa rather than organic cachexia.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - ECG: calculate QTc (normal <450 ms, high risk if >500 ms)
  - Lying/standing BP (postural drop >20 systolic suggests volume depletion)
  - Temperature (hypothermia is a severity marker)
  - Weigh patient in light clothing – do not allow rituals (e.g., empty pockets)
  - Urinalysis: dip for ketones (starvation), pH (vomiting → alkaluria)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (anaemia, leukopenia)
  - U&E (hypokalaemia, hyponatraemia, metabolic alkalosis from vomiting)
  - Bone profile (hypocalcaemia, raised ALP from osteoporosis)
  - Magnesium, phosphate (low pre-referral or falling)
  - Glucose (check for hypoglycaemia)
  - LFTs (mildly raised transaminases – steatosis)
  - Thyroid function (euthyroid sick syndrome – low T3)
  - Cortisol (random >500 nmol/L suggests stress; avoid suppressing with dexamethasone unless Addison’s suspected)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Chest X-ray if suspicion of aspiration or if severely underweight (rib fracture, infection). DEXA scan only if chronic illness (>1 year).
  - **Second-line:** Bone age (if young and growth retardation). Brain MRI only if atypical features (e.g., neurological signs).
- **Special tests:** If purging suspected: stool laxative screen, serum carotene (raised in anorexia from excessive vegetable intake).
- **Diagnostic criteria (DSM-5 summary):**
  - Restriction of energy intake leading to significantly low body weight
  - Intense fear of gaining weight / persistent behaviour to prevent weight gain
  - Disturbance in self-perception of weight/shape; lack of recognition of seriousness of low weight
  - For bulimia: recurrent binge episodes + purging, at least once weekly for 3 months
- **Never-miss test:** **ECG** – every patient with anorexia or bulimia must have QTc measurement before any feeding or electrolyte replacement.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Assess ABC: airway usually fine, but check for aspiration if vomiting/unconscious
  - Secure IV access if unstable (HR <40, BP <70/40, GCS <15, hypoglycaemia)
  - Treat hypoglycaemia: give 50 mL 50% dextrose IV (check K+ first if possible) or 200 mL 10% dextrose if peripheral line only
  - Correct severe electrolyte disturbances:
    - Hypokalaemia (K+ <3.0): IV potassium with cardiac monitoring; max rate 10 mmol/h (central line if >10 mmol/h)
    - Hypomagnesaemia (Mg <0.5): IV magnesium 2–4 mmol/h (e.g., magnesium sulphate 50% 10 mL in 100 mL saline over 1 hour)
    - Hypophosphataemia (PO4 <0.5): IV phosphate 10–20 mmol over 6–12 hours (e.g., sodium phosphate 20 mmol in 500 mL 0.45% saline)
  - Admit to medical ward if:
    - BMI <13
    - Heart rate <40 bpm
    - QTc >450 ms ( >500 ms if symptomatic)
    - Rapid weight loss (>1 kg/week)
    - Electrolyte disturbance requiring monitoring
    - Severe hypotension or hypothermia
    - Suicidal ideation or intention
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - **Refeeding protocol** – start cautiously:
    - Begin at 5–10 kcal/kg/day (max 1000 kcal/d initially); increase by 200–300 kcal every 2–3 days
    - Monitor phosphate, Mg, K+ every 6 hours for first 48 hours, then daily
    - Prophylactic phosphate supplementation (unless level >1.2 mmol/L): e.g., Sandoz Phosphate 500 mg PO/NG QDS; adjust based on levels
  - Daily weight (same time, same clothes, empty bladder)
  - Strict fluid balance – avoid fluid overload (risk of peripheral oedema, heart failure)
  - ECG daily until QTc stable <450 ms
  - Involve liaison psychiatry / eating disorders team within 24 hours
  - Consider NG feeding if patient refuses oral intake and BMI <13 or rapid decline – but only with consent (or MHA if capacity lost)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Specialist multidisciplinary care (dietitian, psychologist, physician)
  - Cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT-E) for bulimia; family-based treatment for adolescents with anorexia
  - Monitoring of bone density if amenorrhoea >6 months; consider calcium + vitamin D (1000–1200 mg/day Ca, 800 IU vitamin D)
  - Gradual supervised refeeding until healthy weight achieved
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** If clinically dehydrated (postural BP drop, dry mucous membranes) – oral fluids only unless unable to swallow; avoid IV fluids if possible due to risk of overload. If IV required: 0.9% saline at 50–100 mL/h (max 1.5 L/d) with strict monitoring
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Assess risk – low BMI is NOT protective; many guidelines recommend prophylactic LMWH (e.g., enoxaparin 20 mg SC daily) if immobile or previous VTE, but weigh bleeding risk (coagulopathy from liver dysfunction). Follow local policy.
  - **Diet / NBM status:** Start oral intake as soon as safe (low-calorie, frequent small meals). NBM only if severe gastrointestinal symptoms (gastric dilatation, pancreatitis).
  - **Observation frequency:** 1–2 hourly observations for first 12 hours (HR, BP, temp, glucose), then 4 hourly once stable.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line for bulimia (if indicated after medical stabilisation):** [Prescribe only under specialist guidance]
  - **Fluoxetine** 20–60 mg PO once daily (NICE recommends 60 mg daily for bulimia; start at 20 mg and increase weekly). Check ECG before starting (QTc risk if severe electrolyte disturbance).
- **Common alternatives:**
  - **Olanzapine** (2.5–10 mg PO nocte) – may be used off-licence for weight gain in anorexia; monitor glucose and lipids.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Avoid SSRIs if QTc >500 ms or untreated electrolyte abnormality
  - Olanzapine: avoid in patients with baseline dysrhythmia or prolonged QTc
  - MAOIs: absolutely contraindicated in patients with impulsivity (risk of overdose)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Fluoxetine: reduced clearance in hepatic impairment – start 10 mg daily
  - Olanzapine: dose adjustment not usually needed but avoid in severe liver disease
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Starting a high dose of IV potassium without checking Mg first** – hypomagnesaemia prevents K+ stabilisation and risks arrhythmia.
  - **Starting refeeding without prophylactic phosphate** – precipitates refeeding syndrome.
  - **Giving a benzodiazepine for agitation to a severely underweight patient** – risk of respiratory depression.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Refeeding syndrome (life-threatening)
  - Cardiac arrhythmias (VT, torsades, bradycardia)
  - Hypokalaemia / hypomagnesaemia / hypophosphataemia
  - Gastric dilatation / rupture (especially after binge-purge)
  - Pancreatitis, aspiration pneumonia
  - Seizures (hypoglycaemia, electrolyte imbalance)
- **Chronic:**
  - Osteoporosis / osteopenia (especially in anorexia with amenorrhoea)
  - Infertility, growth retardation (if adolescent)
  - Dental erosion, loss of teeth (bulimia)
  - Chronic kidney disease (from hypokalaemia, hypovolaemia)
  - Oesophageal rupture (Mallory-Weiss tear) from repeated vomiting

### Prognosis
- Anorexia nervosa: mortality 5–10% over 10 years (one of the highest psychiatric mortality rates); about 50% recover fully, 20–30% remain partial, 10–20% chronic severe.
- Bulimia: lower mortality (~1% per year), better recovery rates with early treatment (70% remission over 5 years).

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Start the refeeding protocol before correcting all electrolytes** – do not delay feeding while correcting hypophosphataemia; use parallel supplementation.
- **Always order an ECG and measure QTc before starting any medication** (including SSRIs, antiemetics – avoid ondansetron as it prolongs QTc).
- **Check orthostatic BP in every patient** – a drop >20 mmHg is a marker of volume depletion and need for careful fluid management.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Never start high-calorie refeeding (e.g., full meal plan) in severe anorexia** – that precipitates refeeding syndrome; always start low (5–10 kcal/kg).
- **Do not ignore bradycardia (HR 40–50) as “normal” in anorexia** – it is a sign of relative adrenal insufficiency and can decompensate with stress.
- **Do not discharge a patient with a QTc >500 ms** – they need continuous ECG monitoring and senior review until resolved.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Palpitations, syncope, or near-syncope (risk of arrhythmia)
  - Inability to keep down any food/bland fluids for >6 hours
  - Severe abdominal pain or vomiting blood (Mallory-Weiss or rupture)
  - Worsening suicidal thoughts or self-harm
- **Follow-up:**
  - Liaison with local eating disorders team within 1–2 weeks
  - GP review of weight, mood, and ECG (if electrolyte abnormalities were present)
  - Dietetic appointment for structured meal plan

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call crash team if:** NEWS2 ≥7, GCS <9, HR <35, BP <60 systolic, new onset arrhythmia (e.g., torsades)
- **Call senior (Reg/Consultant) immediately if:**
  - QTc >500 ms (or any QTc prolongation >460 ms with symptoms)
  - Phosphate <0.5 mmol/L or rapidly declining despite supplementation
  - Seizure, loss of consciousness, or suspected refeeding syndrome
  - Suspected gastric rupture (surgical emergency)
  - Suicidal intent or attempt (liaise psychiatry)
- **General thresholds:**
  - NEWS2 >5 or lactate >2 mmol/L with no clear cause
  - GCS drop by ≥2 points
  - Heart rate <40 or >130 (in the context of starvation)
